skill.name: prototype
skill.description: Build throwaway logic or UI prototypes that answer design questions before production work begins. The skill guides agents to choose a terminal state prototype or a browser UI variant prototype.
skill.summary: Build disposable prototypes to test logic, state, and UI decisions before committing to production code.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are markdown guidance, inline examples, or fenced pseudocode. No malicious intent, prompt injection, credential access, network exfiltration, or executable payload was found. The remaining risk is low because the skill asks agents to create throwaway files, task-runner commands, and UI routes in a host project.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Detections Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby and shell backtick findings are inline markdown examples, terminal command suggestions, or framework pseudocode. They do not execute when the skill is loaded, but generated prototypes may add runnable commands to a host project.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are prose, inline markdown, or fenced pseudocode. No executable skill file, install hook, or automatic command invocation was found.

low_findings.title: ANSI Escape And Weak Crypto Alerts Are Formatting False Positives
low_findings.description: The hex and weak cryptography detections occur in prose and terminal formatting examples. No encryption, hashing, decoding, or obfuscated payload behavior is present in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious hex value is an ANSI terminal style example, not hidden code. The other flagged lines are natural language or UI guidance, not cryptographic routines.

low_findings.title: Keystroke Reader Is Local TUI Input Guidance
low_findings.description: The keylogger keyword detection maps to instructions for a user-driven terminal prototype. It describes reading local keystrokes to update in-memory prototype state, with no persistence or exfiltration sink.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding section describes an interactive local TUI loop. No hidden background capture, file logging, network transmission, or credential targeting is present.

low_findings.title: Prototype Files Can Touch Project Runtime Surfaces
low_findings.description: The skill asks agents to add throwaway files, task-runner entries, UI routes, and notes near production code. This is expected for a prototype skill, but generated changes should be reviewed before merge.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly instructs file and route creation plus runnable command setup. The intent is legitimate, but the behavior can affect repository state.

low_findings.title: Environment Access Is Limited To Production Gating
low_findings.description: The environment access finding is a NODE_ENV check for hiding a prototype switcher in production. No evidence found for reading secrets, enumerating environment variables, or sending values elsewhere.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact environment reference is scoped to production detection. There is no network sink or sensitive variable name in the reviewed context.

content.value_statement: Teams often commit to designs before testing the hard questions. This skill creates disposable logic or UI prototypes that reveal problems early.
actual_capabilities: Chooses between a logic prototype and a UI prototype based on the design question.
Guides creation of a terminal prototype for state transitions and data model checks.
Guides creation of browser UI variants switchable through a URL parameter.
Encourages use of existing project tooling and one clear run command.
Requires visible prototype state so users can compare behavior after each action.
Prompts the agent to capture the decision and remove or absorb prototype code later.
limitations: Generated prototype code is disposable and not production ready.
The skill intentionally skips tests and complete error handling for speed.
It needs enough project context to choose routes, tooling, and component patterns.
Prototype routes and scripts should be reviewed before they are merged.

use_cases.title: Validate State Machines
use_cases.description: Create a small terminal app that lets a developer push state through edge cases by hand.

use_cases.title: Compare UI Directions
use_cases.description: Build several distinct page variants on one route so a product team can choose a direction.

use_cases.title: Test Product Assumptions
use_cases.description: Use a disposable prototype to learn whether a workflow feels right before production work starts.

prompt_templates.title: Start A Simple Logic Prototype
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a state or data model question.
prompt_templates.prompt: Prototype this state model as a small terminal app. Let me trigger actions and see the full state after each action.

prompt_templates.title: Try Three UI Directions
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for visual design exploration.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create three different UI variants for this page on the existing route. Add a switcher so I can compare them in the browser.

prompt_templates.title: Probe An Edge Case
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for a focused business logic check.
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a throwaway prototype that tests what happens when this workflow receives invalid, repeated, and out-of-order actions.

prompt_templates.title: Prototype And Capture A Decision
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request that includes cleanup and decision documentation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Prototype the options, give me one run command, and leave a short notes file with the question, findings, and cleanup path.

output_examples.input: Prototype a checkout state machine before we implement it.
output: A local terminal prototype with actions for adding items, applying discounts, paying, and canceling.
A visible state view after every action so invalid transitions are easy to spot.
A short note describing which transitions need production handling.

output_examples.input: Show me several possible layouts for the settings page.
output: Three route variants that use different layout structures and information hierarchy.
A bottom switcher that updates the variant through the URL parameter.
Cleanup guidance for keeping the chosen design and deleting the rest.

best_practices: State the prototype question before generating files so the result answers one clear problem.
Keep prototype state in memory unless persistence is the exact question being tested.
Delete losing variants and throwaway shells after the team captures the decision.
anti_patterns: Do not treat generated prototype code as production implementation.
Do not connect prototypes to real mutations unless the user explicitly requests that risk.
Do not make UI variants that differ only by color, wording, or small spacing changes.

faq.question: What kind of prototype does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates either a terminal logic prototype or browser UI variants, depending on the question being tested.

faq.question: Does it produce production-ready code?
faq.answer: No. It produces disposable code meant to answer a design or behavior question quickly.

faq.question: Can it work inside an existing application?
faq.answer: Yes. The UI branch prefers existing routes and project conventions when a suitable host page exists.

faq.question: Does it require a specific framework?
faq.answer: No. It tells the agent to use the host project language, routing, and tooling.

faq.question: Why does the audit mention environment access?
faq.answer: The skill recommends a production environment check to hide a prototype switcher from end users.

faq.question: How should teams finish a prototype session?
faq.answer: They should capture the learning, keep the chosen idea if useful, and remove throwaway code.
